query_text:
VLM inference latency scales with visual token count but most
tokens are redundant; how can we identify and remove or merge
low-information visual tokens at inference without retraining
the encoder?

round_records.method:
We propose an inference-time pipeline that transforms a large set of frozen visual tokens into a compact, text-conditioned memory without changing the vision encoder or retraining. First, run the frozen vision encoder as usual to obtain N patch tokens. Before decoding begins (or after the first 1–2 generated tokens), perform a cheap probing pass that uses the model’s existing cross-attention to accumulate a per-token importance score by summing attention mass to each visual token across layers/heads and weighting by the token’s value-norm. In parallel, build a spatially-regularized similarity graph in the visual feature space (nearest-neighbor links constrained by patch adjacency). This gives two signals: token salience (text-conditioned) and token redundancy (feature-similarity + spatial proximity).

Next, adaptively choose a target visual budget r << N based on a global image-dependency gauge: compute a prompt-dependency measure once every few decoding steps by comparing the next-token distribution with and without the visual prompt (e.g., using a symmetric f-divergence). When dependency is high (e.g., early steps, fine-grained queries), keep a larger budget; as dependency fades during generation, shrink r more aggressively. Given r, prune the lowest-salience tokens while enforcing coverage: retain at least one representative per connected region in the similarity graph via farthest-point sampling in feature space, ensuring small but important objects are not lost. Then merge the remaining tokens into r “supertokens” by attention-aware aggregation: for each cross-attention layer/head, form new keys/values as convex combinations of the original tokens within each cluster, using normalized weights proportional to their accumulated attention mass (fallback to uniform if no mass yet). Replace the visual KV cache with these r per-layer supertoken KV tensors and continue decoding.

For later decoding stages, optionally switch from token clusters to a tiny set of virtual conditioning slots that approximate the effect of all visual tokens. Construct M << r virtual slots per cross-attention layer by solving a per-instance low-rank approximation of the token-space: compute the top-M components of the attention-weighted token covariance for keys and values, and instantiate virtual K,V as these components scaled to match the first and second moments of the original K,V under current attention. Inject these virtual slots as a fixed “visual prefix memory” so subsequent steps attend to a constant, tiny set, eliminating further attention over spatial tokens while preserving conditioning.

To keep quality stable, employ a conservative guardrail: after each pruning/merging event, evaluate the dependency gauge on the next step; if it drops below a user-specified threshold (indicating visual influence was over-removed) or the next-token log-probability degrades beyond a small tolerance, partially roll back by restoring the top-k pruned tokens (by salience) or increasing M. This feedback loop converges to the smallest token budget that maintains the model’s current reliance on the image for the given prompt.
round_records.rationale:
The key to reducing VLM latency is to shrink the cross-attention’s visual KV memory online, but only where the image contributes little to the current next-token distribution. The dependency measure provides an annotation-free, per-step signal of how much the model still needs the image; using it to schedule pruning avoids brittle, one-size-fits-all budgets. Within a given budget, attention-weighted clustering preserves the tokens that matter for the prompt while merging redundant, spatially similar patches, which directly cuts the number of visual keys/values without modifying the encoder.

Finally, once the model’s reliance on the image fades, compressing the visual memory to a handful of virtual conditioning slots replaces many tokens with a compact prefix-style representation. This maintains the conditioning pathway with constant-time cross-attention while avoiding retraining by using per-instance low-rank approximations derived from the model’s own attention statistics.

inspired_by.borrowed_aspect: prompt dependency measure to quantify image influence
token aggregation via spatially-aware scaling and merging
zoom-like reshaping to control sequence length with spatial priors
replace many visual tokens with a small set of prefix conditioning slots

round_records.novelty_reasoning:
The proposed method combines several mechanisms: (1) attention+value-norm importance scoring for visual tokens (seen in SparseVLM, VL-Cache, TopV, AdaptInfer), (2) spatially-regularized similarity graph with coverage via farthest-point sampling (partially in SAINT, AdaptPrune, Libra-Merging, but not with farthest-point coverage guarantees), (3) a prompt-dependency measure (f-divergence between conditioned/unconditioned distributions) to adaptively schedule the token budget across decoding steps (the PDM concept from the inspired seed paper, but not combined with token pruning/merging), (4) virtual conditioning slots via per-instance low-rank approximation of attention-weighted token covariance as a visual prefix memory (not found in any search result), and (5) a rollback guardrail that restores pruned tokens if quality degrades (not found). The individual components (attention-based pruning, similarity-based merging, dependency measures) are known, but the specific combination — especially the dependency-gauge-driven adaptive budget scheduling integrated with token merging, plus the virtual conditioning slots via low-rank covariance approximation, plus the rollback guardrail — has no direct published match. Searches returned no paper combining the dependency measure with token compression scheduling, and no paper using low-rank covariance-based virtual slots as a visual prefix memory. This matches the Score 4-5 range; the virtual conditioning slots mechanism is genuinely novel and not surfaced, but the overall pipeline combines several known mechanisms with two new named components (dependency-gauge scheduling and virtual conditioning slots), placing it at Score 4-5. Given the two novel named components not found in literature, this edges toward 5, but the heavy reuse of known building blocks (attention scoring, similarity merging, PDM concept) keeps it at 4.

Prior works:
  - SparseVLM (2024): text-guided training-free visual token pruning with attention scoring and token recycling/merging for VLMs
  - VL-Cache (2024): modality-aware KV cache compression with layer-adaptive budget allocation for VLM inference
  - TopV (2025): token pruning as optimization with feature similarity, spatial distance, and central distance cost function
  - AdaptPrune/Multi-Cue Adaptive Visual Token Pruning (2025): attention + spatial + similarity signals for training-free VLM token pruning
  - Multi-Modal Hallucination Control by Visual Information Grounding (2024): prompt dependency measure (PDM) comparing conditioned vs. unconditioned distributions to quantify visual grounding
  - SAINT (2025): graph-based token similarity pruning framework for ViTs and VLMs
  - Libra-Merging (2025): importance-redundancy trade-off with spatial interval selection and information compensation token merging
prior_works: SparseVLM (2024): text-guided training-free visual token pruning with attention scoring and token recycling/merging for VLMs
VL-Cache (2024): modality-aware KV cache compression with layer-adaptive budget allocation for VLM inference
TopV (2025): token pruning as optimization with feature similarity, spatial distance, and central distance cost function
AdaptPrune/Multi-Cue Adaptive Visual Token Pruning (2025): attention + spatial + similarity signals for training-free VLM token pruning
Multi-Modal Hallucination Control by Visual Information Grounding (2024): prompt dependency measure (PDM) comparing conditioned vs. unconditioned distributions to quantify visual grounding
SAINT (2025): graph-based token similarity pruning framework for ViTs and VLMs
Libra-Merging (2025): importance-redundancy trade-off with spatial interval selection and information compensation token merging

round_records.method:
We propose Token Triage and Prototype Merge (T2PM), a training-free, plug-in module that sits between the frozen vision encoder and the language model. Given N visual tokens from the frozen encoder, T2PM selects a small, text-relevant core set and merges the rest into a handful of prototypes so the language model only attends to K≪N tokens. The module is budget-aware (target K) and fully deterministic, adding only light-weight matrix ops and nearest-neighbor assignments.

Stage A — Hierarchical screening with text conditioning: First, compute a cheap, unconditional saliency for each visual token. If encoder attention maps are available (e.g., CLS→patch), use attention rollout to score tokens; otherwise combine simple cues (token feature norm, local non-maximum suppression over the spatial grid, and self-similarity centrality to downweight near-duplicates). Retain the top C≈2–3K candidates. Next, text-condition the scores without changing any weights: project visual tokens into the language space using the model’s frozen vision-to-LLM adaptor, form a compact text query embedding from the prompt (e.g., mean of its first few hidden states), and compute token–text affinities (dot products or a one-step cross-attention). Rank candidates by a blend of unconditional saliency, text affinity, and uniqueness (e.g., distance from nearest neighbor to spare rare/outlier details). To avoid O(C log C) sorts and all-pairs comparisons, perform m right-to-left adjacent comparison passes over the candidate list, swapping neighbors when the right token has higher blended score; after a few passes, take the first K′ (K′≤K) as the kept core tokens.

Stage B — Prototype merging of redundant tokens with uncertainty safeguards: Anchor K′ prototypes at the kept tokens. Assign every remaining token to its nearest prototype by cosine similarity in the frozen encoder’s token space (or pre-projection space), and aggregate features via similarity-weighted averaging; aggregate positions likewise (weighted by assignment) to update positional encodings and store each prototype’s “mass” (number of absorbed tokens). For safety, compute a simple assignment uncertainty per discarded token (e.g., margin between top-1 and top-2 cosine, or the entropy of the normalized similarities). Tokens with ambiguous assignments or unusually low similarity to all prototypes are promoted back into the kept set until the final budget K is met. The output to the LLM is the K prototype embeddings (kept tokens plus merged prototypes), their updated positions, and optional mass scalars that can be used to rescale value vectors in the first cross-attention.

Optional light refinement in the LLM’s first cross-attention: After the first fusion layer updates the multimodal hidden states, re-evaluate the blended scores using the updated text-conditioned affinities for only the bottom-r fraction of tokens and perform one additional adjacent-pass refinement and merge. This preserves training-free operation while allowing a last-mile correction if the initial text-conditioned scores changed after fusion. The refinement is inexpensive and typically reduces tokens further without notable quality loss.

Practical notes: T2PM does not alter or retrain the vision encoder; it only reads its outputs (and, when available, attention weights) and replaces the raw N tokens with K prototypes before multimodal fusion. For captioning or open-ended prompts with weak text priors, T2PM falls back to unconditional saliency plus uniqueness to select core tokens. All steps are implementable with batched matrix ops and k-NN assignments; the overhead is small relative to the savings from reducing the language model’s cross-attention over visual tokens.
round_records.rationale:
Inference latency in VLMs scales roughly linearly with the number of visual tokens the language model must attend to. Many of those tokens are redundant due to spatial smoothness and repeated textures, while only a subset is both unique and relevant to the prompt. T2PM reduces the token count post-encoder—where latency pressure is highest—by combining a text-aware selector (to keep what matters) with prototype merging (to compress redundancy), all without modifying or retraining the encoder.

Hierarchical screening makes the selection fast and robust: a cheap unconditional pass trims obvious redundancies, and a lightweight, training-free text-conditioning step prioritizes prompt-relevant regions. Efficient adjacent-pair refinements avoid expensive global sorts. Prototype-based merging preserves information by aggregating similar tokens with position-aware weighting, while an uncertainty safeguard protects small or rare details from being collapsed. The result is a compact, semantics-preserving token set that substantially reduces cross-modal attention cost with minimal implementation overhead and no changes to the frozen encoder.

inspired_by.borrowed_aspect: plug-and-play selector before a heavier reasoning model
efficient adjacent-pair re-ranking to find top-k with few comparisons
prototype-based latent similarity with confidence to guide merging
hierarchical screening to progressively prune candidates

round_records.novelty_reasoning:
The searches surfaced several closely related papers. FSR (Focus-Scan-Refine) is the closest match: it combines visual importance + instruction relevance (Stage A of T2PM), complementary context scanning (uniqueness criterion in T2PM), and similarity-based merging with score-weighted aggregation (Stage B of T2PM). ICCTP uses instruction-guided clustering as prototypes for pruning. ConsensusDrop fuses vision encoder saliency with cross-modal attention and token merging. ResPrune uses text-conditioned subspace reconstruction for token selection. However, T2PM introduces several specific design choices not found jointly in any single paper: (1) the uncertainty safeguard that promotes ambiguously-assigned tokens back into the kept set based on cosine margin/entropy, (2) the adjacent-comparison pass refinement (borrowed from GLIMPSE-style RTL passes) applied to visual token ranking, (3) the optional post-first-LLM-layer refinement step, and (4) the mass scalar for rescaling value vectors. FSR is the strongest overlap — it has the same three-stage structure (focus=text-conditioned selection, scan=uniqueness/diversity, refine=similarity merging). The combination is very close to FSR, though T2PM adds the uncertainty promotion mechanism and the LLM-layer refinement. This matches the Score 3 anchor: combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint application to this problem family, where FSR already pairs the same core mechanisms for the same problem.

Prior works:
  - Focus-Scan-Refine (FSR) (2026): Three-stage training-free VLM token pruning combining visual importance + instruction relevance selection, diversity-based scanning, and similarity-weighted merging — directly parallels T2PM's Stage A + Stage B structure.
  - Instruction-Guided Cross-Modal Clustering Token Pruning (ICCTP) (2026): Training-free, plug-in method using text-conditioned clustering (noun anchors as cluster centers) to prune visual tokens in VLMs — overlaps with T2PM's text-conditioned prototype merging.
  - ConsensusDrop (2026): Fuses vision encoder saliency with cross-modal attention and token merging — overlaps with T2PM's unconditional saliency + text affinity fusion.
  - ResPrune (2026): Text-conditioned subspace reconstruction for training-free visual token pruning — overlaps with T2PM's text-conditioned selection stage.
prior_works: Focus-Scan-Refine (FSR) (2026): Three-stage training-free VLM token pruning combining visual importance + instruction relevance selection, diversity-based scanning, and similarity-weighted merging — directly parallels T2PM's Stage A + Stage B structure.

Instruction-Guided Cross-Modal Clustering Token Pruning (ICCTP) (2026): Training-free, plug-in method using text-conditioned clustering (noun anchors as cluster centers) to prune visual tokens in VLMs — overlaps with T2PM's text-conditioned prototype merging.
ConsensusDrop (2026): Fuses vision encoder saliency with cross-modal attention and token merging — overlaps with T2PM's unconditional saliency + text affinity fusion.
ResPrune (2026): Text-conditioned subspace reconstruction for training-free visual token pruning — overlaps with T2PM's text-conditioned selection stage.

round_records.method:
SCOPE-V: Spectral Certificate-On-demand Pruning and Expansion is a training-free, plug-in pipeline that replaces most visual tokens with a small, text-conditioned spectral basis plus a few leverage-score exemplars, and then adapts this basis online across early language layers with error certificates and on-demand unpruning. It avoids retraining the vision encoder and circumvents per-token K/V projections by aggregating pruned-token contributions in the pre-projection space.

Step 1 — Query-conditioned spectral condensation: From the frozen vision encoder, collect N tokens X ∈ R^{N×d} and 2D coordinates. Form a text summary vector t by averaging language-side queries at the first vision–language cross-attention call. Compute relevance weights w_i = softmax(λ · (x_i W_k · t)), and build a weighted, mean-centered matrix X_w. Run a fast Frequent-Directions-style sketch (or incremental PCA) to obtain a rank-r orthonormal basis U_r and singular values σ. Construct r spectral tokens s_j = σ_j u_j and keep m exemplar tokens with highest text-conditioned leverage l_i = ||(I − U_r U_r^T)x_i||^2 · w_i. For pruned tokens, compute nonnegative assignment coefficients to the spectral tokens via projection magnitudes a_{ij} = |x_i^T u_j| normalized across j.

Step 2 — Value-preserving aggregation via RBF barycentric routing: To avoid computing W_v and W_k on every pruned token, aggregate them before projection. Build r residual buckets S_j = ∑_{i pruned} π_{ij} x_i, where π_{ij} blends projection-based a_{ij} with a compact RBF kernel over 2D coordinates and feature cosine, ensuring locality. Only the r spectral tokens and m exemplars are passed through the VLM’s linear maps: K,V for spectral tokens are K(s_j), V(s_j) and a residual correction R_j = W_v S_j. Each spectral token’s value becomes Ṽ_j = V(s_j) + α · R_j, where α rescales mass to conserve the summed L1 norm of values from its assigned pruned tokens. Exemplar tokens pass through unchanged. This yields r + m tokens total, while the contributions of all other tokens are preserved linearly without per-token projections.

Step 3 — Discretely-online refinement with prototype-certified unpruning: Run the language model with the condensed set for a short layer interval (e.g., the first one or two cross-attn layers). After the interval, compute a deterministic, prototype-margin certificate over the spectral tokens: treat {s_j} as prototypes in the attended key space and measure, per language position, the entropy or top-2 cosine margin of its attention over prototypes. If uncertainty is high and attention gradients concentrate on a prototype p, on-demand unprune the top-b pruned tokens with largest residual energy toward p (e.g., highest π_{ip}·w_i or leverage). Update the spectral basis discretely-online by rerunning a small-rank Frequent Directions on X using new relevance weights proportional to the actually observed attention, smoothed by an uncertainty-aware EMA; keep r fixed and swap in any new directions with largest singular mass. Proceed in short intervals, repeating certificate→optional unpruning→basis refresh, until the token budget or a low-uncertainty state is reached.

Implementation notes: SCOPE-V is architecture-agnostic and needs only to (a) read the first cross-attn queries to form t, (b) replace the vision token list with {spectral tokens + exemplars}, and (c) inject on-demand tokens at layer boundaries. Complexity is O(N d r) once per image to form U_r and O((r + m) d) per head thereafter. Aggregation is K-nearest in the 2D grid, typically K ≤ 4, and does not require per-token K/V. The method degrades gracefully: if no text is available (captioning, OCR-free modes), it falls back to unconditional spectral condensation (w_i uniform); if the certificate remains high-entropy, the system expands toward the full set under the same interface.
round_records.rationale:
The latency bottleneck in VLMs scales with visual token count, but most tokens lie close to a low-dimensional subspace conditioned on the current instruction. Rather than pruning/merging by clustering or attention heuristics, SCOPE-V constructs a text-conditioned spectral basis that linearly spans the information needed by the language queries and replaces thousands of tokens with r directions plus a handful of outlier exemplars. Because attention projections are linear, aggregating pruned-token contributions before the V map preserves their effect with a single projection per basis element, yielding large compute savings without retraining.

Two additional ingredients make the approach robust and novel. First, a deterministic, prototype-margin certificate over the spectral tokens detects when the condensed representation is ambiguous for the current textual queries, and triggers targeted, on-demand unpruning only where evidence is lacking. Second, the spectral basis is updated discretely-online across early language layers using the attention actually observed, with uncertainty-aware EMA smoothing, so the condensed representation tracks what the model needs as reasoning unfolds—without ever peeking ahead or modifying the frozen encoder. Together, these components provide an anytime, error-aware token reduction mechanism that operates entirely at inference time and is complementary to existing token-selection and merging schemes.

inspired_by.borrowed_aspect: discretely-online updates over short intervals for causal, real-time refinement
RBF interpolation from sparse points to reconstruct a continuous field
uncertainty-guided EMA to stabilize online updates

round_records.novelty_reasoning:
The searches reveal that existing training-free VLM token compression methods (SparseVLM, TokenCarve, SwiftVLM, etc.) use attention-based pruning/merging with text guidance, but none combine: (1) a text-conditioned spectral basis (Frequent Directions/incremental PCA) replacing tokens with rank-r spectral directions plus leverage-score exemplars, (2) pre-projection RBF barycentric aggregation of pruned-token contributions into residual buckets to avoid per-token K/V projections, and (3) a prototype-margin certificate driving on-demand unpruning with discretely-online spectral basis refresh via uncertainty-aware EMA. No surfaced paper pairs spectral condensation (as actual replacement tokens) with leverage-score exemplars, pre-projection value aggregation, and certificate-triggered adaptive unpruning for VLM visual token reduction. The individual mechanisms (PCA/SVD for compression, text-conditioned pruning, adaptive layer-wise token selection) appear separately, but their specific combination as proposed is not found in any search result. This matches the Score 5 anchor: a new named mechanism (spectral certificate tokens + pre-projection barycentric routing + prototype-margin certificate unpruning) with no end-to-end match surfaced.

Prior works:
  - SparseVLM (2024): text-guided training-free visual token pruning with token recycling — closest to text-conditioned pruning component but uses attention scores, not spectral basis
  - TokenCarve (2025): information-preserving two-stage token compression for VLMs — related plug-and-play pruning+merging but no spectral basis or certificate unpruning
  - SwiftVLM (2026): cross-layer token bypass with re-evaluation — related to adaptive layer-wise token selection but no spectral basis or pre-projection aggregation
  - CoVSpec (2026): low-rank dependency used in token pruning for VLMs — uses low-rank as one signal among others, not as replacement spectral tokens with pre-projection aggregation
prior_works: SparseVLM (2024): text-guided training-free visual token pruning with token recycling — closest to text-conditioned pruning component but uses attention scores, not spectral basis
TokenCarve (2025): information-preserving two-stage token compression for VLMs — related plug-and-play pruning+merging but no spectral basis or certificate unpruning
SwiftVLM (2026): cross-layer token bypass with re-evaluation — related to adaptive layer-wise token selection but no spectral basis or pre-projection aggregation
CoVSpec (2026): low-rank dependency used in token pruning for VLMs — uses low-rank as one signal among others, not as replacement spectral tokens with pre-projection aggregation

This yields r + m tokens total, while the contributions of all other tokens are preserved linearly without per-token projections.

Step 3 — Discretely-online refinement with prototype-certified unpruning: Run the language model with the condensed set for a short layer interval (e.g., the first one or two cross-attn layers). After the interval, compute a deterministic, prototype-margin certificate over the spectral tokens: treat {s_j} as prototypes in the attended key space and measure, per language position, the entropy or top-2 cosine margin of its attention over prototypes. If uncertainty is high and attention gradients concentrate on a prototype p, on-demand unprune the top-b pruned tokens with largest residual energy toward p (e.g., highest π_{ip}·w_i or leverage). Update the spectral basis discretely-online by rerunning a small-rank Frequent Directions on X using new relevance weights proportional to the actually observed attention, smoothed by an uncertainty-aware EMA; keep r fixed and swap in any new directions with largest singular mass. Proceed in short intervals, repeating certificate→optional unpruning→basis refresh, until the token budget or a low-uncertainty state is reached.

Implementation notes: SCOPE-V is architecture-agnostic and needs only to (a) read the first cross-attn queries to form t, (b) replace the vision token list with {spectral tokens + exemplars}, and (c) inject on-demand tokens at layer boundaries. Complexity is O(N d r) once per image to form U_r and O((r + m) d) per head thereafter. Aggregation is K-nearest in the 2D grid, typically K ≤ 4, and does not require per-token K/V. The method degrades gracefully: if no text is available (captioning, OCR-free modes), it falls back to unconditional spectral condensation (w_i uniform); if the certificate remains high-entropy, the system expands toward the full set under the same interface.
d_loop.final_rationale:
The latency bottleneck in VLMs scales with visual token count, but most tokens lie close to a low-dimensional subspace conditioned on the current instruction. Rather than pruning/merging by clustering or attention heuristics, SCOPE-V constructs a text-conditioned spectral basis that linearly spans the information needed by the language queries and replaces thousands of tokens with r directions plus a handful of outlier exemplars. Because attention projections are linear, aggregating pruned-token contributions before the V map preserves their effect with a single projection per basis element, yielding large compute savings without retraining.

Two additional ingredients make the approach robust and novel. First, a deterministic, prototype-margin certificate over the spectral tokens detects when the condensed representation is ambiguous for the current textual queries, and triggers targeted, on-demand unpruning only where evidence is lacking. Second, the spectral basis is updated discretely-online across early language layers using the attention actually observed, with uncertainty-aware EMA smoothing, so the condensed representation tracks what the model needs as reasoning unfolds—without ever peeking ahead or modifying the frozen encoder. Together, these components provide an anytime, error-aware token reduction mechanism that operates entirely at inference time and is complementary to existing token-selection and merging schemes.

final_inspired_by.borrowed_aspect: discretely-online updates over short intervals for causal, real-time refinement
RBF interpolation from sparse points to reconstruct a continuous field
uncertainty-guided EMA to stabilize online updates

novelty.reasoning: Method A's strongest novel element is the combination of Frequent-Directions-style spectral sketching with text-conditioned leverage scores, RBF barycentric routing for pre-projection aggregation, and a prototype-margin certificate with on-demand unpruning — particularly the idea of aggregating pruned token contributions *before* the projection matrices to avoid per-token K/V computation, and the discretely-online basis refresh guided by uncertainty certificates. Method B's strongest novel element is the adaptive budget scheduling via a prompt-dependency gauge (comparing next-token distributions with/without visual prompt using f-divergence), combined with a rollback guardrail and the transition from spatial supertokens to virtual conditioning slots via low-rank covariance approximation. Both methods are complex combinations, but Method A introduces a more distinctly original mechanism: the pre-projection spectral aggregation with barycentric routing and certificate-driven unpruning is a harder-to-find combination in the literature, whereas Method B's components (attention-based salience, graph-based coverage, KV cache merging, low-rank approximation) are individually closer to existing work on token pruning/merging, with the dependency gauge being the most novel element but less architecturally distinctive than Method A's spectral framework.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode is redundant visual tokens at inference causing high latency, where the goal is to identify and remove/merge low-information tokens without retraining the encoder. Both methods address this directly. Method A uses attention-based salience scoring combined with spatial similarity graphs to prune and merge tokens into supertokens, with an adaptive budget controlled by a text-image dependency gauge — each step traces directly from the redundancy problem to a concrete mechanism. Method B uses spectral condensation (Frequent Directions sketch) to replace tokens with a low-rank basis plus leverage-score exemplars, with value-preserving aggregation before projection and online certificate-based unpruning — also tracing directly to the redundancy problem. Both engage the failure mode head-on. However, Method A's mechanism is more directly tied to the specific failure mode as stated: it explicitly identifies low-information tokens via attention mass (salience) and feature similarity (redundancy), then removes or merges them, with a traceable causal pathway at each step. Method B's spectral approach is also valid but introduces additional complexity (online basis refinement, certificate-based unpruning) that addresses peripheral concerns beyond the core failure mode, making the causal pathway slightly less direct.

coherence.reasoning: Method A has a coherence weakness in its "virtual conditioning slots" stage: it proposes constructing virtual K,V slots by solving a per-instance low-rank approximation of the "attention-weighted token covariance" and then instantiating virtual K,V as components "scaled to match the first and second moments of the original K,V under current attention." This is underspecified — matching moments of a distribution via PCA components is not a standard, well-defined procedure, and the transition from spatial supertokens to these virtual slots introduces a paradigm shift mid-pipeline that lacks clear implementation steps. The rollback guardrail is also somewhat vague about how partial restoration interacts with the already-merged KV cache. Method B has a coherence weakness in Step 2: it claims to avoid per-token K/V projections by aggregating in pre-projection space, but then computes W_v S_j (a projection of the aggregated residual bucket), which still requires applying the linear map — the claimed computational saving is partially illusory. More seriously, the "discretely-online refinement" in Step 3 involves rerunning Frequent Directions mid-forward-pass at layer boundaries and swapping spectral basis directions, which is an unusual and technically demanding operation to perform inside a standard VLM forward pass without architectural hooks. However, each individual step in Method B is more precisely defined (specific formulas, clear inputs/outputs), and the overall pipeline, while complex, is more consistently specified than Method A's later stages. Method A's rollback mechanism and virtual slot construction are less coherently defined than Method B's corresponding components.
